And that's the second kind of interpretability where we simplify the models or tend to use simpler models. Then we have behavioral interpretability where you do some sensitivity testing. But it's really, I think of it as a way to zoom in and understand how models interpret the input and output data. So why do we need this far? So the encoders are short SAEs. The real issue is polysemanticity. And I think polysemanticity means the model reuses parameters for different concepts. So there's a lot more concept than parameters. For example, Astrophysics and Michael Jordan can be represented by the same neuron. They mean absolutely different things.but they're actually reusing the parameters. So then disentangling these features is very difficult. So what do we do? We create this microscope to zoom in into residual stream of a network. And see, you know, by making it sparse, you're effectively disentangling the features inside the model. And on the right side here, you can see an interesting example. It's a fairly simple example. And you ask LLM, what is the capital of France? I mean, I think the answer is most LMs these days, you don't need to have billions of parameters to get that answer, that the answer is Paris. But what's interesting, when you apply microscope or sparse on encoder to residual stream, what you're starting to see is these features forming. And the features are actually relevant to your question, because when we ask what is the capital of France, the features you're seeing is things associated with French culture, French flag, Eiffel Tower, maybe wine, baguette. And here is an example whereYou can also think of the sparse order encoder as a tap, a tap into residual stream. And this tap disentangles or distributes the residual stream into particular features. It knows that there's an animal, but then it starts seeing what type of animal it is. And it now tries to understand that there's a car and a tree. Feature or vector of numbersand you scale it up and down, you increase the scale of this vector. So what are we doing about it? We are looking into the way to monitor agent reasoning during execution of the workflow steps. We're looking at the signals to predict whether there's a reasoning fault. We're using feature activations and live diagnostic signal that something might be wrong with an agent or something wrong with execution. For example, we could detect tool called misfires. So agents call various tools, you know, they go to the web, they go to internal databases, but that could be, you know, we could be executing the wrong query in the database. So what we could do, we can actually detect it live and either stop execution or we can actually correct the execution. So I think here it's, you know, I think the benefits are enormous. It's not just monitoring or compliance. This is actually about saving costs because agents, as you might be aware, are consuming a lot of tokens. So what you could be doing is here on a diagram.below, you can be looking at agent execution, looking inside the model, and you could be pausing or adjusting the agents to do the right thing. And that not only builds confidence in your agentic system, but it also actually potentially saves on compute. So now we're kind of coming to the end of our talk. We're going to talk a little bit about what tools we used. I think we used a fairly common software stack. We used PyTorch and NVIDIA CUDA, rich software libraries. And we used an interesting, going down the software stack, we used NVIDIA Launchpad, which is an amazing remote system where you can... On a lot of different experiments. You get access to underlying hardware, which were H100 GPUs. There were eight of them. And that allowed us, this stack allowed us to do a lot of experimentation and push our research forward, especially where compute requirements were higher than an average workstation would allow.So let's talk a little bit about limitations. So I think limitations are, we are still in early days of understanding these models. Number one requirement, and I would say it's less of a limitation, more of a requirement, you need to have open wave models. You cannot look inside of the black boxes, not in your control. So I think the alternatives are if you don't have access to open weight models or you don't have the hardware, you can always ask your favorite vendor to provide interpretability tools. I think it's very fair as a, you know, if you are a large user of LLM models, you should be able to ask for interpretability tools to build confidence in applications you're building. The other limitation of our research is right now, as Hiram alluded to, we're limited to single layers. We're looking at particular layers. Obviously, circuits, as described, was the future of this space. Unfortunately, circuits are still very hard. If you think about it, you have multiple layers and you're putting them together into a circuit. That's not an obvious or simple task to do. But this is obviously a limitation that we're working to address in the future. It's also very compute heavy. The cross coders to train them, order encoder across all layers is very compute intensive. And we have...limited kind of metrics around interpretability. How do you detect kind of your, you know, what are these features and how do you translate it to metrics that help you evaluate the model? This is very much an ongoing research. What are the final thoughts? So still we feel that mechanistic interpretability, looking inside the model, might be underrated, especially in high-stake domain like finance. And we think that this is something which should be must-have in finance and also high-stakes domain. Why? Because going back to some of the slides that I talked about earlier, 85% of the use cases are still stuck in POC state, valid state. So knowing deeper or getting insight into the models might unlock and give us confidence so that we are able to even unlock new use cases and scale adoption across our industry. It will definitely build regulator trust because now we can say that, hey, at least we are able to go inside and look and see, understand some part of the model, and which will again allow for wider deployment. And I talked about the subprime crisis in one of the slides before. If we understand the model really well inside out, it might avoid or it might help us avoid one of the financial crisis going forward. And that's why, that's one of the reasons we are very much focused on this interpretability research.Thank you. Thank you.

that look inside the large language models or the AI models.

These are even more ingrained in the workflows of the organization.
And if we don't understand it well, we might be at loss again.

Another reason why interpretability is needed is this.
I'll let you guys go through this slide.
The idea here is that if we are not satisfied with the answer we get from chat GPT or AI models, we just try to run it again and again till we reach a really good state, a really good answer.
It's good.
Everybody does that.

Like finance.
You don't want to do that again and again.
And you need to have more deterministic output.
You need to understand the models really well.
Now, talking about the interpretability type,
It's not something which is new.
This is something which has been there since always.
We all have used machine learning and AI models in finance, in other use cases, or in other domains, for things like fraud detection, trading, and whatnot.
And we have used all of these interpretability.
The first one is feature attribution.
There are a bunch of models, approaches, Shai, Lime, integrated gradients.
So what we do here is, hey, what percentage of this fraud is attributed to the location of the person?
So the attribution comes from the feature attribution based interpretability.
Then we have model simplification, where we simplify the model or try to use simpler models.
And that's one of the reasons why in finance you will see we focus on the simpler models because there is more explainability.
You can see the variables.
And that's the second kind of interpretability where we simplify the models or tend to use simpler models.
Then we have behavioral interpretability where you do some sensitivity testing.
What we are doing here is we are kind of shocking the inputs in order to see the impact of the output.
It gives you the marginal impact of the input and the output.
And then we have visual explanation where we see the input maps which are kind of causing or impacting the output and there are a bunch of techniques there as well.
And then we have mechanistic interpretability.
It stands out.
Why?
Because all of these approaches you see, they...
look at the inputs and the outputs from outside.
They don't go deeper inside the model.
And that's where the McIntyre or mechanistic interpretability comes into picture.
Okay, so going deeper into mechanistic interpretability, something I talked about in the previous slide,
We call it neuroscience of generative AI.
Some people also call it MRI for AI.
The idea here is, unlike other types of interpretability techniques, we go deeper into the neurons and the layers, and we reverse engineer that.
So on the right-hand side, you see an example of diabetes control.
If you think about other types of interpretability, what we are doing there is you are trying to analyze what person eats, what's the pattern.
And through that, you are trying to understand the overall pattern and find out the solution.
It's different in mechanistic interpretability or McIntyre.
What you're trying to do is you're trying to go inside the brain of the person.
You are trying to find out the neurons or the layer, the part of the brain which is responsible for sugar craving and not just find it, but you can also reduce its influence.
So it's very powerful.
It's something you can control from inside.
And you don't have to do the same prompting, for example, do the same thing again and again because you know the reason and you can control it.

Do we need mech and terp or mechanistic invertibility?

But I think it's very hard to trust this, if you will, black boxes, so to speak.
Without really understanding how they work.
And this was the real reason why we wrote and researched this space.

I think post financial crisis, there was a very heavy investment in financial services and what we call model risk management.

Fundamentally,
Post-financial crisis, the trust in models went almost to zero.
We couldn't trust our models to measure our risk, to produce valuations.
So we introduced a process where we have independent functions inside banks that review
and verify the models.

We're a heavily regulated industry, and the regulators need to trust that our systems are safe of the financial systems.

It caused a systemic risk.

We cannot just leave it as a black box.

Finance is a high-stakes AI application.
You know, losses are, could be monumental, and the government's requirements are higher versus potentially other industries.
Therefore, we need to invest more and understand better how these black boxes work.
What tools do we use for actually doing this interpretability analysis?
And I'll dive a little bit of what I call
microscope of LLMs.
It's a little bit of a technical name, sparse order encoders.
I think it's a cool, you know, machine learning term.
But it's really, I think of it as a way to zoom in and understand how models interpret the input and output data.
So why do we need this far?
So the encoders are short SAEs.
The real issue is polysemanticity.
And I think polysemanticity means the model reuses parameters for different concepts.
So there's a lot more concept than parameters.
For example,
Astrophysics and Michael Jordan can be represented by the same neuron.
They mean absolutely different things.
but they're actually reusing the parameters.
So then disentangling these features is very difficult.
So what do we do?
We create this microscope to zoom in into residual stream of a network.
And see, you know, by making it sparse, you're effectively disentangling the features inside the model.
And on the right side here, you can see an interesting example.
It's a fairly simple example.
And you ask LLM, what is the capital of France?
I mean, I think the answer is most LMs these days, you don't need to have billions of parameters to get that answer, that the answer is Paris.
But what's interesting, when you apply microscope or sparse on encoder to residual stream, what you're starting to see is these features forming.

And the features are actually relevant to your question, because when we ask what is the capital of France, the features you're seeing is things associated with French culture, French flag, Eiffel Tower, maybe wine, baguette.
So you start believing that the model actually...
Disentangling and relating to the right features to answer that the capital of France is Paris and not anything else.
Let's move to the next slide.
And here is an example where
You can also think of the sparse order encoder as a tap, a tap into residual stream.
And this tap disentangles or distributes the residual stream into particular features.
And this particular example, we have a, you know, layer two residual stream feeding into sparse order encoder.
Sparsely encoder starts disentangling dogs and cats.
It knows that there's an animal, but then it starts seeing what type of animal it is.
And it now tries to understand that there's a car and a tree.
And after that, order encoder completes its stream disentangling.
It passes the data back to layer three.
And that way you can start tapping into multiple layers of the neural network.
And seeing what are different features or concepts are forming across different parts of the network.
And that therefore builds confidence that what's happening inside, so to speak, black box, is actually correct behavior.
And it builds confidence that the models, the way we use them, are actually going to be used properly without errors or faults.
Now, I already talked about Sparsoto encoder.
What you see as the features or the concepts doesn't come like this.
It comes like numbers.
So we need to convert those into human readable label.
The simple thing that we do here is we run this parse autoencoder by many sentences, many keywords, many articles, and many tokens.
Where they activate the most, that's the label we give to them.
So for example, in this case, one of the features
is activating quite a lot on the keyword London and we give this feature the name as references to London.

Now it doesn't sound, it's not as easy as it sounds because think about the number of concepts we have, think about the number of features we have, so it has to run by multiple sentences, multiple tokens and it's research in itself.
Now quickly touching upon some of the other interpretability tools that we also looked into when we were doing this research, apart from this sparse autoencoder that Ari talked about.
One of them is called LogitLens.
What we do here is we
just look at each and every layer of the large language model and see what's the result of the next in terms of the next token for a given prompt from different layer.
So here we see the cat at which is kind of the list of the tokens.
We're trying to look for the next token.
The layer one gives
token which is not very intuitive and as we go deeper into the large language models we see we go deeper
into the layers of the LLMVC, it becomes more and more intuitive.
So in a way, it kind of gives you which layer adds the maximum impact or maximum value, but it doesn't go much deeper beyond the layer level.
So we thought it's slightly on a higher level, and we have an example of this in the paper as well.
The next tool, which is kind of the folks in the McIntyre focus on this tool quite a lot these days, which is called Linear Probe.
The idea is it works very similar to the prediction model.
You can predict whether, let's say, the price will go up or down if you think about financial context.
But the difference is that the prediction model is inside or baked into the layers and the neurons of the LLM.
So what you have in order to train this model, you have a sample where you have, let's say, the news articles and then the price going up or down.
That's what you train this probe with.
So the internal layers of the internal neurons of the LLM, they kind of are used for the prediction.
Those are the variables.
Those are much richer in terms of the context.
And once you have trained the model, you can predict whether the price will go up or down.

and so on and so forth.
So these were a couple of tools I would say it's kind of maybe simpler compared to sparse autoencoder, but something we have even more complex compared to sparse autoencoder,
pretty new, these are called circuits.
So the LLMs have multiple layers and the information flows through them in form of some complex circuits.
So you can think of this as a combination of logic lens and sparse autoencoder.
And what happens here is it gives you the feature for the different tokens that activate across different layers and how those features kind of combine together to give you the output.
So for example, if you have this input token, the capital of state containing Dallas is.

Capital, state, Dallas, move to the next one where the features kind of combine.
There's more complex interactions between the feature.

and then sparse autoencoder.

But what's interesting is that what Hiram mentioned before is that usually the residual stream and the sparse autoencoders, you know, data flows, you don't really understand what these features are.

You can try to label them by passing a lot of text through a neural network and seeing what activations are happening and infer that most likely this feature is maybe, you know, French culture or feature it's a car or a tree.
There's a different way you can actually have the model explain what this feature is.
And it's something kind of fascinating personally to me is you actually ask a model to describe the feature.
But then what you do, you steer the feature.
What steering the feature or the concept means is that you actually start modifying the network using this autoencoder.
And what happens is you take a particular feature that you don't have a label for.
For example, it's French culture.
What you do is you take that...
Feature or vector of numbers
and you scale it up and down, you increase the scale of this vector.
Once you increase the scale, you ask, once you increase the scale, you ask the neural network, what is the meaning of word X?
And you prompt it saying the meaning of word X is.
And once you tap with your special sparse autoencoder or SAE into your neural network, and you jack up
the scale of a particular feature, the model describes itself what the features are.
So if I find a feature, and I can go through a list of all the features that we have in a sparse autoencoder, and one by one
increase the scale and ask the model what the feature X is, the model will explain itself what this means.
I feel like it's a fascinating concept of letting model to self-intervene itself.
One other interesting concept that we're able to do once we went through all of the features of a sparse order encoder and we've managed to
label every feature available to us, some features are very similar to each other.
So what you can actually do, you can start clustering these features.

You have a concept, Jim.
political boundaries, regions, countries.

So what's amazing is these features inside the models, they're related to each other and you can actually cluster them by vector similarity.
